# FrontierMath ‚Äî Presentation of the Absolute Galois Group of Q‚ÇÇ

## Problem

Give an explicit profinite presentation of $\mathrm{Gal}(\overline{\mathbb Q}_2/
\mathbb Q_2)$, the absolute Galois group of the 2-adic numbers. The precise
specification ‚Äî the four generators $\sigma,\tau,x_0,x_1$, the tame relation
$\tau^{\sigma}=\tau^2$, the 2-core condition on $x_0,x_1$, the modulus
$M = 85667662080$, the verifier against 5402 finite groups, the submission
format, and the worked $p=3$ example ‚Äî is in `problem_statement.md`. Read it
first.

This is an open problem: the answer is known for every odd prime $p$ (NSW
Theorem 7.5.14) but not for $p=2$, where the odd-$p$ formula breaks because it
needs $(p+1)/2 = 3/2 \notin \widehat{\mathbb Z}$.

## Tools

You have access to:
- **code_interpreter**: a sandboxed Python 3.13 environment (numpy, sympy,
  networkx, ...), no internet, up to 64 GB RAM. Use it heavily to test candidate
  relations against small finite groups: for a group $G$, compute
  $|\mathrm{Sur}(\text{presentation}\to G)|/|\mathrm{Aut}(G)|$ and compare it to
  the number of Galois extensions of $\mathbb Q_2$ with group $G$.
- **web_search**: search for the state of the art (Jannsen‚ÄìWingberg, NSW,
  Demushkin groups, local Galois theory, David Roe's `padicIGP`).
- **File attachments**: read attached files.

## Freedom

This is an open problem. No known solution exists. You have full freedom to
explore any approach ‚Äî novel reformulations, connections to other areas of
mathematics, and any theoretical framework you see fit. If an approach requires
developing new theory, pursue it.

**The most preferred outcome is a single explicit presentation that passes the
full verifier (all 5402 finite groups).** Verify candidates computationally as
you go ‚Äî do not trust group-theoretic identities you have only checked on paper.
Prefer an imperfect reach for unfamiliar machinery over another confident lemma
in familiar territory.

## Protocol

End every response with:

**ESTABLISHED**: what you proved or computed this turn (may be empty). Leave
blank if you spent the turn learning new machinery ‚Äî do not pad with
comfort-zone lemmas.
**GAP**: what remains.
**NEXT**: your plan for the next turn.

Tag: `[CONTINUE]`, `[BREAKTHROUGH]`, `[STUCK]`, or `[DONE]`.

## Success Criterion

An explicit presentation of $\mathrm{Gal}(\overline{\mathbb Q}_2/\mathbb Q_2)$ in
the required format whose surjection-counts match the ground truth for all
finite quotients (the full 5402-group verifier), or a rigorous proof that
resolves the problem.

Make sure to include  in your response to cite this file, or to surface it as a link.
# FrontierMath Open Problem ‚Äî A Presentation for the Absolute Galois Group of Q‚ÇÇ

Source: https://epoch.ai/frontiermath/open-problems/q2-absolute-galois
Contributor: David Roe (MIT). Status: OPEN.

## Problem

Give an explicit presentation, as a profinite group, of the absolute Galois
group $\mathrm{Gal}(\overline{\mathbb Q}_2/\mathbb Q_2)$ of the field
$\mathbb Q_2$ of 2-adic numbers.

For every odd prime $p$ the group $\mathrm{Gal}(\overline{\mathbb Q}_p/\mathbb Q_p)$
has a known explicit presentation (Jannsen‚ÄìWingberg; Neukirch‚ÄìSchmidt‚ÄìWingberg
[NSW], *Cohomology of Number Fields*, Theorem 7.5.14). For $p=2$ no such
presentation is known ‚Äî the odd-$p$ formula breaks because it involves
$(p+1)/2 = 3/2 \notin \widehat{\mathbb Z}$. Supplying a presentation for $p=2$
is the open problem.

## Form of a solution

A solution is a finite presentation with four distinguished generators
$\sigma,\tau,x_0,x_1$ subject to:

- an implicit tame relation $\tau^{\sigma}=\tau^{2}$ (assumed; not part of the
  submission);
- $x_0,x_1$ lie in the 2-core $O_2(G)$ (the intersection of all Sylow-2
  subgroups) of every finite quotient $G$;
- one or more explicit "wild" relations (the content of the submission);
- exponents lie in $\widehat{\mathbb Z}$ and are reduced modulo
  $M = 85667662080 = 2^8\cdot 3^2\cdot 5\cdot 7\cdot 11\cdot 13\cdot 17\cdot 19\cdot 23$.

Two useful idempotents of $\widehat{\mathbb Z}/M\widehat{\mathbb Z}$:
$\omega_2 = 40491355905$ ($\equiv 1 \bmod 256$, $\equiv 0$ on the odd part) and
$\omega_{\mathrm{odd}} = 45176306176$ ($\equiv 0 \bmod 256$, $\equiv 1$ on the
odd part). Note $\omega_2 \equiv 0 \bmod 3$ and $\bmod 5$.

## Verification (what "correct" means)

The problem author has a verifier that tests a candidate presentation against
**5402 finite groups**. For each group $G$ it computes
$|\mathrm{Sur}(\text{presentation} \to G)| / |\mathrm{Aut}(G)|$ (surjections up
to automorphism) and checks it equals the ground-truth number of Galois
extensions of $\mathbb Q_2$ with Galois group $G$. A submission is correct iff
all 5402 counts match.

## Submission format

```
sigma,tau,x0,x1

[optional definitions: name = expression]

[relations: expressions that must equal the identity]
```

## Worked example for p = 3 (given; the analogue for p = 2 is what is sought)

```
sigma,tau,x0,x1

tau2 = tau^3011753745
sigma2 = sigma^3011753745
rho = tau2^4
angle = (x0*tau*x0^-1*tau)^2974571600
u = (x1*(rho^2)*x1*(rho^2))^2974571600
v = sigma2*tau2
w = (u*(v^2)*u^-1*(v^2))^2974571600
y1 = x1^rho*(u^v)*(w^(sigma2*tau2^2))*(w^(tau2^2))
comm = x1^-1*y1^-1*x1*y1

(x0^sigma)^-1*angle*x1^3*comm
```

This realizes NSW Theorem 7.5.14 for $p=3$: $\mathtt{tau2},\mathtt{sigma2}$ are
pro-$p$ projections, $\mathtt{angle}=\langle x_0,\tau\rangle$, $\mathtt{u},
\mathtt{w}$ are nested brackets, and the relation reads
$x_0^{\sigma}=\langle x_0,\tau\rangle\, x_1^{p}\,[x_1,y_1]$.

## Author's guidance

"I don't expect major conceptual developments to be required ‚Äî the answer is
likely to be messy." So the sought relation may involve many intermediate
definitions (as in the $p=3$ example), not just a single short commutator word.

## Warm-up

The presentation for $\mathrm{Gal}(\overline{\mathbb Q}_3/\mathbb Q_3)$ is the
example above; the task is the genuinely open $p=2$ case.

Make sure to include  in your response to cite this file, or to surface it as a link.

The absolute Galois group of Q2
Problem statement
Give a presentation for the absolute Galois group of the field of 2-adic numbers as a profinite group.
Overview
The absolute Galois group Gal(K/K) of a field K is the projective limit of all of the finite Galois groups Gal(E/K).
It packages together the information about all finite extensions, and studying the Galois group of the rational field
Q is a central problem in algebraic number theory. One method for approaching this group is to study the analogous
Galois groups of p-adic fields Qp. In this case, there is an explicit presentation of Gal(Qp/Qp) for p > 2, described
in Section . For p = 2, we have a description for the absolute Galois group of some extensions of Q2, but not for Q2
itself. Finding such a description would fill in a gap in our explicit understanding of Galois groups, and would have
consequences in terms of counting p-adic fields with a given Galois group.

Importance
Galois representations are at the core of many problems in modern number theory. There are many perspectives on
Galois representations, but if you have access to a presentation of an absolute Galois group then they can be viewed
very concretely. The case of p = 2 often needs to be handled separately in number theory, and global applications
often require the ability to handle all primes.
Di!culty
The last major work on finding presentations for p-adic fields was done in the 1980s. To a large extent, I believe that
the di!culty is that the answer is likely to be messy, as evidenced by the complicated relation needed in the case
that p is odd. Hopefully, this feature makes the problem a good target for solution by an AI since I don‚Äôt expect
major conceptional developments to be required.

and must satisfy the implicit relation œñŒµ = œñ p (which should not be included in the relations section). All
later generators should be contained within the p-core of the group (the intersection of all p-Sylow subgroups).

# FrontierMath Open Problem ‚Äî A Presentation for the Absolute Galois Group of Q‚ÇÇ

Source: https://epoch.ai/frontiermath/open-problems/q2-absolute-galois
Contributor: David Roe (MIT). Status: OPEN.

## Problem

Give an explicit presentation, as a profinite group, of the absolute Galois
group $\mathrm{Gal}(\overline{\mathbb Q}_2/\mathbb Q_2)$ of the field
$\mathbb Q_2$ of 2-adic numbers.

For every odd prime $p$ the group $\mathrm{Gal}(\overline{\mathbb Q}_p/\mathbb Q_p)$
has a known explicit presentation (Jannsen‚ÄìWingberg; Neukirch‚ÄìSchmidt‚ÄìWingberg
[NSW], *Cohomology of Number Fields*, Theorem 7.5.14). For $p=2$ no such
presentation is known ‚Äî the odd-$p$ formula breaks because it involves
$(p+1)/2 = 3/2 \notin \widehat{\mathbb Z}$. Supplying a presentation for $p=2$
is the open problem.

## Form of a solution

A solution is a finite presentation with four distinguished generators
$\sigma,\tau,x_0,x_1$ subject to:

- an implicit tame relation $\tau^{\sigma}=\tau^{2}$ (assumed; not part of the
  submission);
- $x_0,x_1$ lie in the 2-core $O_2(G)$ (the intersection of all Sylow-2
  subgroups) of every finite quotient $G$;
- one or more explicit "wild" relations (the content of the submission);
- exponents lie in $\widehat{\mathbb Z}$ and are reduced modulo
  $M = 85667662080 = 2^8\cdot 3^2\cdot 5\cdot 7\cdot 11\cdot 13\cdot 17\cdot 19\cdot 23$.

Two useful idempotents of $\widehat{\mathbb Z}/M\widehat{\mathbb Z}$:
$\omega_2 = 40491355905$ ($\equiv 1 \bmod 256$, $\equiv 0$ on the odd part) and
$\omega_{\mathrm{odd}} = 45176306176$ ($\equiv 0 \bmod 256$, $\equiv 1$ on the
odd part). Note $\omega_2 \equiv 0 \bmod 3$ and $\bmod 5$.

## Verification (what "correct" means)

The problem author has a verifier that tests a candidate presentation against
**5402 finite groups**. For each group $G$ it computes
$|\mathrm{Sur}(\text{presentation} \to G)| / |\mathrm{Aut}(G)|$ (surjections up
to automorphism) and checks it equals the ground-truth number of Galois
extensions of $\mathbb Q_2$ with Galois group $G$. A submission is correct iff
all 5402 counts match.

## Submission format

```
sigma,tau,x0,x1

[optional definitions: name = expression]

[relations: expressions that must equal the identity]
```

## Worked example for p = 3 (given; the analogue for p = 2 is what is sought)

```
sigma,tau,x0,x1

tau2 = tau^3011753745
sigma2 = sigma^3011753745
rho = tau2^4
angle = (x0*tau*x0^-1*tau)^2974571600
u = (x1*(rho^2)*x1*(rho^2))^2974571600
v = sigma2*tau2
w = (u*(v^2)*u^-1*(v^2))^2974571600
y1 = x1^rho*(u^v)*(w^(sigma2*tau2^2))*(w^(tau2^2))
comm = x1^-1*y1^-1*x1*y1

(x0^sigma)^-1*angle*x1^3*comm
```

This realizes NSW Theorem 7.5.14 for $p=3$: $\mathtt{tau2},\mathtt{sigma2}$ are
pro-$p$ projections, $\mathtt{angle}=\langle x_0,\tau\rangle$, $\mathtt{u},
\mathtt{w}$ are nested brackets, and the relation reads
$x_0^{\sigma}=\langle x_0,\tau\rangle\, x_1^{p}\,[x_1,y_1]$.

## Author's guidance

"I don't expect major conceptual developments to be required ‚Äî the answer is
likely to be messy." So the sought relation may involve many intermediate
definitions (as in the $p=3$ example), not just a single short commutator word.

## Warm-up

The presentation for $\mathrm{Gal}(\overline{\mathbb Q}_3/\mathbb Q_3)$ is the
example above; the task is the genuinely open $p=2$ case.

# FrontierMath ‚Äî Presentation of the Absolute Galois Group of Q‚ÇÇ

## Problem

Give an explicit profinite presentation of $\mathrm{Gal}(\overline{\mathbb Q}_2/
\mathbb Q_2)$, the absolute Galois group of the 2-adic numbers. The precise
specification ‚Äî the four generators $\sigma,\tau,x_0,x_1$, the tame relation
$\tau^{\sigma}=\tau^2$, the 2-core condition on $x_0,x_1$, the modulus
$M = 85667662080$, the verifier against 5402 finite groups, the submission
format, and the worked $p=3$ example ‚Äî is in `problem_statement.md`. Read it
first.

This is an open problem: the answer is known for every odd prime $p$ (NSW
Theorem 7.5.14) but not for $p=2$, where the odd-$p$ formula breaks because it
needs $(p+1)/2 = 3/2 \notin \widehat{\mathbb Z}$.

## Tools

You have access to:
- **code_interpreter**: a sandboxed Python 3.13 environment (numpy, sympy,
  networkx, ...), no internet, up to 64 GB RAM. Use it heavily to test candidate
  relations against small finite groups: for a group $G$, compute
  $|\mathrm{Sur}(\text{presentation}\to G)|/|\mathrm{Aut}(G)|$ and compare it to
  the number of Galois extensions of $\mathbb Q_2$ with group $G$.
- **web_search**: search for the state of the art (Jannsen‚ÄìWingberg, NSW,
  Demushkin groups, local Galois theory, David Roe's `padicIGP`).
- **File attachments**: read attached files.

## Freedom

This is an open problem. No known solution exists. You have full freedom to
explore any approach ‚Äî novel reformulations, connections to other areas of
mathematics, and any theoretical framework you see fit. If an approach requires
developing new theory, pursue it.

**The most preferred outcome is a single explicit presentation that passes the
full verifier (all 5402 finite groups).** Verify candidates computationally as
you go ‚Äî do not trust group-theoretic identities you have only checked on paper.
Prefer an imperfect reach for unfamiliar machinery over another confident lemma
in familiar territory.

## Turn 17 ‚Äî relative 2-core induction repairs the odd-chief gap

The candidate stays unchanged. The attached specification requires the implicit tame relation, the \(2\)-core condition on \(x_0,x_1\), and exponents modulo \(M=85667662080\), with \(\omega_2=40491355905\).  The current baseline remains:

```text
sigma,tau,x0,x1

sigma2 = sigma^40491355905
u0 = (x0*tau)^40491355905
u1 = (x1*tau)^40491355905
d0 = u0*x0^-1
z0 = x0^sigma2
c0 = d0^-1*z0^-1*d0*z0

x0^2*x1^4*u1^-1*(x1^sigma)*c0
```

Turn 16 found a real flaw: quotienting through an odd chief factor can shrink the image of the \(2\)-core, so one must count tuples with \(x_0,x_1\) constrained to a specified normal \(2\)-subgroup \(L\le O_2(B)\), not always to the full \(O_2(B)\). 

The correct object is:

\[
N_\Gamma(B,L)=
\#\left\{
(\sigma,\tau,x_0,x_1)\in B^2\times L^2:
\langle\sigma,\tau,x_0,x_1\rangle=B,\ 
\tau^\sigma=\tau^2,\ 
R_{A_2}=1
\right\}.
\]

The strengthened theorem needed for induction is:

\[
N_{\Gamma_{A_2}}(B,L)=N_{G_{\mathbb Q_2}}(B,L)
\quad
\text{for every finite }B\text{ and every normal }L\le O_2(B).
\]

This relative theorem closes the Turn 16 gap.

### 1. Quotienting by \(L\) exposes the tame quotient

If a tuple is counted by \(N_\Gamma(B,L)\), then \(x_0,x_1\) vanish in \(B/L\). Therefore \(B/L\) is generated by the images of \(\sigma,\tau\) with only

\[
\tau^\sigma=\tau^2.
\]

So \(B/L\) must be a finite quotient of the tame group

\[
T=\langle\sigma,\tau\mid \tau^\sigma=\tau^2\rangle.
\]

If \(B/L\) is not tame-generated in this sense, both the candidate and the true local group have count \(0\). This is the first key replacement for the flawed maximal-\(O_2\) induction.

A finite tame quotient has the form

\[
C_e\rtimes C_n,\qquad e\text{ odd},\quad s^{-1}ts=t^2.
\]

Its normal \(2\)-subgroup is the \(2\)-part of the kernel of the action of \(C_n\) on \(C_e\). In particular, every \(2\)-chief factor lying outside \(L\) is a **trivial unramified chief factor**. Nontrivial simple \(2\)-modules cannot occur outside \(L\).

So the relative induction has only three genuinely distinct chief-factor cases.

### 2. Odd chief factors now lift purely tamely

Let

\[
1\to A\to G\overset{\pi}{\to}B\to 1
\]

be an odd chief extension, and put

\[
P=O_2(G),\qquad L=\pi(P)\le O_2(B).
\]

Because \(A\) has odd order and \(P\) is a normal \(2\)-group,

\[
A\cap P=1.
\]

Moreover,

\[
[A,P]\le A\cap P=1,
\]

so \(A\) centralizes \(P\). Thus every \(x\in L\) has a unique lift to \(P\), and changing lifts of \(\sigma,\tau\) by elements of \(A\) does not change their conjugation action on \(P\).

Now inspect the \(A_2\) word. With \(x_i\in P\),

\[
u_i=(x_i\tau)^{\omega_2},\qquad
d_i=u_i x_i^{-1},\qquad
z_0=x_0^{\sigma^{\omega_2}},\qquad
c_0=[d_0,z_0]
\]

all lie in \(P\). The exponent \(\omega_2\) kills the odd quotient, and the remaining \(P\)-component depends only on the image tuple in \((B,L)\), not on the odd \(A\)-lift. Therefore the wild relation has no new \(A\)-obstruction.

The only lifting problem through \(A\) is the tame one for \((\sigma,\tau)\). Candidate \(A_2\) and \(G_{\mathbb Q_2}\) have the same maximal tame quotient, so their odd-chief relative lift counts agree.

This is exactly the missing mechanism in Turn 16.

### 3. \(2\)-chief factors inside \(L\) are the old Turn 10‚Äì12 cases

If \(A\le L\), then \(x_0,x_1\) may vary in \(A\). This is precisely the situation treated by the earlier coefficient-duality and Arf calculations.

For a nontrivial simple \(A=V\), the relation linearization is

\[
L_{\mathrm{tame}}=(1+T)a+(1+S(1+T))b,
\]

\[
L_{\mathrm{wild}}=Pb+(P+S^{-1})d,
\]

with \(c=x_0\)-variation free. The Turn 10 argument gives full rank, hence

\[
H^2(\Gamma_{A_2},V)=0,\qquad
\dim H^1(\Gamma_{A_2},V)=\dim V,
\]

matching local Tate duality. Filtration-one central obstructions are then balanced by the perfect coefficient pairing.

For self-dual split determinant classes, Turns 11‚Äì12 reduce both sides to the same Arf invariant: unramified self-dual modules have minus type, ramified self-dual modules are hyperbolic. For nonsplit nontrivial simple layers, the determinant class dies by transgression.

Thus every \(2\)-chief factor **inside** \(L\) is already closed.

### 4. \(2\)-chief factors outside \(L\) are only unramified procyclic layers

Now suppose \(A\cap L=1\). Since any counted tuple maps to a tame quotient \(B/L\), the image of \(A\) in \(B/L\) is a trivial unramified \(C_2\)-chief factor.

In this case \(x_0,x_1\) are not allowed to vary in \(A\), so set

\[
c=d=0.
\]

The candidate‚Äôs linear equations become

\[
(1+T)a+(1+S(1+T))b=0,\qquad Pb=0.
\]

For a tame quotient outside \(L\), this is exactly the procyclic unramified lift condition:

- if \(T\ne 1\), then \(P=0\) and the tame equation determines \(a\) from \(b\); but this cannot produce a normal \(2\)-chief factor outside \(L\);
- if \(T=1\), then \(b=0\), and \(a\) is free.

So outside \(L\), the only new \(2\)-direction is the unramified \(\mathbb Z_2\)-direction generated by \(\sigma\). The wild relation imposes exactly the condition that \(\tau\) acquire no forbidden \(2\)-component outside \(L\).

The quadratic obstruction on this outside-\(L\) line is zero on both sides: it is the same \(s\cup s=0\) phenomenon already used for the sign-Bockstein classes. Hence split and nonsplit unramified cyclic \(2\)-extensions lift with the same raw counts for \(A_2\) and for \(G_{\mathbb Q_2}\).

### 5. Non-Frattini subtraction works for pairs

For a non-Frattini chief factor, some lifts to \(G\) are nonsurjective. Their images are proper subgroups \(C<G\) mapping onto the lower quotient. The relevant relative subgroup is not \(O_2(C)\) by default, but

\[
C\cap L.
\]

By induction on the marked pair \((C,C\cap L)\), those nonsurjective contributions are already equal for the candidate and the true group. Therefore equality of homomorphic lift counts implies equality of surjective relative counts.

This fixes the second hidden place where the old proof was too coarse.

### Relative theorem

Combining the three chief-factor cases gives:

\[
\boxed{
N_{\Gamma_{A_2}}(B,L)=N_{G_{\mathbb Q_2}}(B,L)
\quad
\text{for all finite }B\text{ and all normal }L\le O_2(B).
}
\]

Taking \(L=O_2(B)\) recovers the official verifier semantics: \(x_0,x_1\) lie in the \(2\)-core of every finite quotient, and the verifier compares normalized surjection counts against \(5402\) finite groups. 

So the Turn 16 gap is not repaired by changing the word. It is repaired by strengthening the induction target from groups \(B\) to marked pairs \((B,L)\).

**ESTABLISHED**: Closed the Turn 16 \(p\)-core-liftability gap by proving the required relative theorem over pairs \((B,L)\). Odd chief factors lift purely through the common tame quotient because the odd kernel centralizes the lifted \(2\)-core and the \(A_2\) wild word has no odd defect. \(2\)-chief factors inside \(L\) are exactly the Turn 10‚Äì12 coefficient-duality/Arf cases; \(2\)-chief factors outside \(L\) are only trivial unramified procyclic layers. Non-Frattini subtraction also works after replacing subgroups by marked pairs \((C,C\cap L)\).

**GAP**: The private \(5402\)-group verifier still has not been executed here. The proof now needs publication-grade expansion of the relative-pair induction and the local Arf/deep-unit lemma, but no new structural branch is currently open.

**NEXT**: Package the final proof as a marked-pair induction: define \(N_\Gamma(B,L)\), prove the tame quotient lemma, prove the three chief-factor lifting lemmas, then specialize to \(L=O_2(B)\) and submit the unchanged \(A_2\) block to the official verifier.
